findings.description: User-controlled class name from HTTP request parameter flows directly to Class.forName() and newInstance() in ConsoleOperations.createFeature(), enabling arbitrary class loading and instantiation
findings.code_snippet:
Class<?> strategyClass = Class.forName(strategy);
                    FlippingStrategy fstrategy = (FlippingStrategy) strategyClass.newInstance();
findings.reasoning: The `strategy` parameter is read directly from the HTTP request via `req.getParameter(STRATEGY)` (line 104) with no validation or allowlist check. This value is then passed to `Class.forName(strategy)` at line 107, followed immediately by `strategyClass.newInstance()` at line 108. This creates a complete exploit chain: an attacker can supply any class name on the classpath, causing it to be loaded and instantiated. There is no type filtering beyond the ClassCastException that would be thrown if the class doesn't implement FlippingStrategy - but the instantiation still occurs first. Any class with a no-arg constructor that has side effects in its constructor or static initializer block can be exploited.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: req.getParameter(STRATEGY) at line 104 -> strategy variable -> Class.forName(strategy) at line 107 -> strategyClass.newInstance() at line 108
evidence.notes: Direct unsanitized HTTP parameter to Class.forName() + newInstance() with no allowlist validation

exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the ff4j embedded console endpoint (may require authentication depending on deployment)
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary class instantiation / Remote code execution via gadget classes

                FlippingStrategy fstrategy = (FlippingStrategy) strategyClass.newInstance();

The strategy string is passed to `Class.forName(strategy)` at line 248, followed by `strategyClass.newInstance()` at line 249, with no validation or allowlist check. An attacker can supply any class name available on the server classpath to trigger arbitrary class instantiation.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: req.getParameter(STRATEGY) at line 303 -> updateFlippingStrategy() arg -> Class.forName(strategy) at line 248 -> strategyClass.newInstance() at line 249
evidence.notes: Called from the update feature flow, same pattern as createFeature

findings.description: User-controlled FlippingStrategy class name from REST API JSON body flows to Class.forName() and newInstance() in MappingUtil.instanceFlippingStrategy(), enabling arbitrary class loading
findings.code_snippet:
Class<FlippingStrategy> clazz = (Class<FlippingStrategy>) (classLoader == null ? Class.forName(className) : classLoader.loadClass(className));
            FlippingStrategy flipStrategy = clazz.newInstance();
findings.reasoning: The `className` parameter originates from user-controlled JSON input received via REST API. The flow is: FeatureResource.upsertFeature() receives JSON (line 108) → extracts FlippingStrategyApiBean.getType() (line 124) → passes to MappingUtil.instanceFlippingStrategy(id, flipApiBean.getType(), initparams) → Class.forName(className) at line 183 → clazz.newInstance() at line 184. There is no class name validation or allowlist. The same sink is reached via FeaturesController via HTTP request parameter (line 265). Complete exploit chain confirmed by Query 5.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: REST API JSON body -> FeatureResource.upsertFeature() line 108 -> FlippingStrategyApiBean.getType() line 124 -> MappingUtil.instanceFlippingStrategy() -> Class.forName(className) line 183 -> clazz.newInstance() line 184. Also: FeaturesController req.getParameter(STRATEGY) line 249 -> MappingUtil.instanceFlippingStrategy() line 265 -> same sink
evidence.notes: Multiple entry points (REST API PUT /features/{uid} and web console) both reach this sink

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends PUT request to REST API with an arbitrary class name as the flippingStrategy type field, causing Class.forName() + newInstance() to be called on any server-side class
exploit_payload.prerequisites: ROLE_WRITE permission on the REST API (may be unauthenticated depending on deployment)
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary class instantiation / Remote code execution via gadget classes

findings.description: User-controlled property type class name from REST API JSON body or web console HTTP parameter flows to Class.forName() and newInstance() in PropertyFactory.createProperty(), enabling arbitrary class loading
findings.code_snippet:
Constructor<?> constr = Class.forName(pType).getConstructor(String.class, String.class);
            ap = (Property<?>) constr.newInstance(pName, pValue);
findings.reasoning: The `pType` parameter originates from user-controlled input via multiple paths: (1) REST API PUT /features/{uid} - PropertyApiBean.getType() from JSON body (line 98 in PropertyApiBean → line 163 in PropertyFactory); (2) REST API PUT /properties/{name} - PropertyApiBean.asProperty() → PropertyFactory.createProperty(); (3) Web console ConsoleOperations.createProperty() - req.getParameter("pType") line 228 → PropertyFactory.createProperty() line 232; (4) Web console ConsoleOperations.updateProperty() - req.getParameter("pType") line 161 → PropertyFactory.createProperty() line 182. No validation or allowlist is applied. The full exploit chain calls Class.forName(pType).getConstructor(...).newInstance(...) to instantiate an arbitrary class with the provided name and value strings.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Multiple entry points: (1) FeatureResource line 108 JSON body -> PropertyApiBean.asProperty() -> PropertyFactory.createProperty(name, type, ...) -> Class.forName(pType) line 163; (2) PropertyResource line 103 -> same path; (3) ConsoleOperations.createProperty() req.getParameter('pType') line 228 -> PropertyFactory.createProperty() line 232 -> Class.forName(pType) line 163; (4) ConsoleOperations.updateProperty() req.getParameter('pType') line 161 -> PropertyFactory.createProperty() line 182 -> Class.forName(pType) line 163
evidence.notes: Requires class to have a (String, String) constructor, slightly reduces attack surface but not a sufficient mitigation

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends PUT request with an arbitrary class name as the property type field, causing Class.forName() + newInstance(name, value) on any server-side class with a (String, String) constructor
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary class instantiation / Remote code execution via gadget classes

findings.description: User-controlled class name value flows to Class.forName() in PropertyClass.fromString(), enabling arbitrary class loading via REST API property value deserialization
findings.reasoning: PropertyClass.fromString() is called when the system needs to convert a string value to a Class object for a PropertyClass-typed property. The flow traced by CodeQL shows: REST API (FeatureResource line 108, PropertyResource line 103/167) → PropertyApiBean.asProperty() (line 97-98) → PropertyFactory.createProperty() (line 158, 169) → Property.setValueFromString() (line 126-127) → PropertyClass.fromString() (line 75, 77) → Class.forName(v). Also reached via PropertiesController (line 156, 159) and JdbcPropertyStore (line 176, 185). When a property of type PropertyClass is set with a user-controlled value, the string value itself becomes a class name passed to Class.forName() with no validation.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: REST API JSON body property value -> PropertyApiBean.asProperty() -> PropertyFactory.createProperty() -> Property.setValueFromString() -> PropertyClass.fromString() -> Class.forName(v). Also via PropertiesController HTTP parameter and JdbcPropertyStore reading from DB that was previously written by attacker.
evidence.notes: This represents a secondary loading mechanism - when an existing PropertyClass-typed property value is set to an attacker-controlled class name string

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates or updates a PropertyClass-typed property with a malicious class name as the value, triggering Class.forName() when the property is accessed or deserialized
exploit_payload.prerequisites: ROLE_WRITE permission on REST API, PropertyClass type must be accepted
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary class loading via property value deserialization

findings.description: Class.forName() in ConsoleServlet.init() uses servlet init parameter from web.xml configuration, not user-controlled HTTP input
findings.code_snippet: Class<?> c = Class.forName(className);
findings.reasoning: The `className` is obtained from `servletConfig.getInitParameter(PROVIDER_PARAM_NAME)` - this is a servlet init parameter defined in web.xml at deployment time, not from any user-controlled HTTP request parameter. An attacker would need to modify the server's web.xml deployment descriptor to influence this value, which requires privileged server-level access. This is not a remotely exploitable vulnerability under normal conditions and represents an expected configuration pattern for servlet-based frameworks.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: servletConfig.getInitParameter() is from web.xml deployment configuration, not HTTP request
evidence.notes: Standard servlet initialization pattern - not user-controlled

findings.description: File upload in ConsoleServlet.doPost() parses attacker-supplied XML config files, passing class names from the file content to Class.forName() in XmlParser, enabling arbitrary class loading
findings.code_snippet:
if (filename.toLowerCase().endsWith("xml")) {
                            importFile(getFf4j(), new XmlParser().parseConfigurationFile(item.getInputStream()));
findings.reasoning: ConsoleServlet.doPost() accepts multipart file uploads (line 300) and directly passes the uploaded file's InputStream to `new XmlParser().parseConfigurationFile()`. The XmlParser then reads class names from the XML content at lines 388 (property type: `Class.forName(optionalType).getConstructor(...)`) and 443 (flip strategy: `Class.forName(clazzName).newInstance()`). An attacker who can access the ff4j console can upload a crafted XML file containing arbitrary class names to trigger Class.forName() + newInstance() with no validation. This is a complete exploit chain from file upload to arbitrary class instantiation.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Multipart file upload -> XmlParser.parseConfigurationFile(item.getInputStream()) -> XmlParser.parseFlipStrategy() line 443: Class.forName(clazzName).newInstance() and XmlParser.parsePropertiesTag() line 388: Class.forName(optionalType).getConstructor()
evidence.notes: No authentication check visible in this code path - any user with access to console can upload a file

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Craft malicious XML file
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" ?>
<ff4j xmlns="http://www.ff4j.org/schema/ff4j">
  <features>
    <feature uid="exploit">
      <flipstrategy class="com.malicious.EvilStrategy"/>
    </feature>
  </features>
</ff4j>

# Upload via multipart POST:
curl -X POST http://target/ff4j-console \
  -F 'FLIPFILE=@malicious.xml;type=text/xml'
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker uploads a crafted XML configuration file to the ff4j console, causing Class.forName().newInstance() to be called on an attacker-specified class
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the ff4j embedded console file upload endpoint
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary class instantiation via file upload / Remote code execution

findings.description: Class.forName() in XmlParser.parseFlipStrategy() loads class names read directly from XML configuration file content, enabling arbitrary class instantiation when attacker-supplied XML is parsed
findings.code_snippet: flipStrategy = (FlippingStrategy) Class.forName(clazzName).newInstance();
findings.reasoning: XmlParser.parseFlipStrategy() reads `clazzName` from the XML element attribute `FLIPSTRATEGY_ATTCLASS` (the class attribute of flipstrategy tag), then calls `Class.forName(clazzName).newInstance()` with no validation. When this parser is invoked via ConsoleServlet file upload or any other path that accepts user-supplied XML files, the class name from the XML content flows directly to Class.forName(). The complete exploit chain from ConsoleServlet file upload is confirmed.

evidence.code_context:
String clazzName = nnm.getNamedItem(FLIPSTRATEGY_ATTCLASS).getNodeValue();
flipStrategy = (FlippingStrategy) Class.forName(clazzName).newInstance();
evidence.dataflow_analysis: ConsoleServlet file upload -> XmlParser.parseConfigurationFile() -> parseFlipStrategy() -> Class.forName(clazzName).newInstance()
evidence.notes: Also reachable from application startup if XML config file is tampered with

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# XML content with malicious class name:
<flipstrategy class="com.malicious.EvilStrategy" />
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies XML with malicious flipstrategy class attribute causing arbitrary class instantiation
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to supply XML to the ff4j parser (via file upload or tampered config)

findings.description: Class.forName() in XmlParser.parsePropertiesTag() loads class names read directly from XML configuration file content, enabling arbitrary class instantiation via file upload
findings.code_snippet:
Constructor<?> constr = Class.forName(optionalType).getConstructor(String.class, String.class);
                    ap = (Property<?>) constr.newInstance(name, value);
findings.reasoning: XmlParser.parsePropertiesTag() reads the property type attribute from XML, maps it via MappingUtil.mapPropertyType(), then calls Class.forName(optionalType).getConstructor(String.class, String.class).newInstance(name, value) with no class name validation. When invoked from ConsoleServlet file upload, an attacker can specify any class with a (String, String) constructor as the property type.

evidence.notes: mapPropertyType does some substitution but does not restrict to known-safe classes

exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to ff4j console file upload

findings.description: Class.forName() + newInstance() in PropertiesParser.parseProperties() and parseFeatures() loads class names from .properties config files, exploitable if attacker can supply config file content
findings.code_snippet: // Class.forName() + newInstance() on class name from parsed .properties file content
findings.reasoning: PropertiesParser reads class names from .properties configuration file content and calls Class.forName().newInstance() on them (lines 234 and 311). If the application allows users to upload .properties files (similar to the XML upload in ConsoleServlet) or if the config file can otherwise be tampered with by an attacker, this is exploitable. The primary question is whether there's a user-accessible path to provide .properties file content - the ConsoleServlet upload handler only accepts XML files (checks for .xml extension), but the parser itself is reachable if other upload vectors exist. Requires manual review to confirm exploitability based on deployment.

evidence.code_context: Complete exploit chain confirmed by Query 5 (UUID 20260328222539): Class.forName() at line 234 followed by newInstance() in PropertiesParser.parseProperties and at line 311 in parseFeatures
evidence.notes: ConsoleServlet upload only accepts XML not properties files - need to verify other entry points

findings.code_snippet: // Class.forName() + newInstance() on class name from parsed YAML file content
findings.reasoning: YamlParser.parseFlipStrategy() calls Class.forName().newInstance() on class names read from YAML config files (line 254). The ConsoleServlet upload handler does not accept YAML files (only XML), but if other code paths allow user-supplied YAML to be parsed, this would be exploitable. Requires manual review to confirm accessible entry points.

evidence.code_context: Complete exploit chain confirmed by Query 5 (UUID 20260328222539): Class.forName() at line 254 followed by newInstance() in YamlParser.parseFlipStrategy
evidence.notes: Need to verify if YAML upload is accessible to users

findings.description: Externally-controlled class name from HTTP parameter passed to Class.forName() and newInstance() in createFeature
findings.code_snippet:
Class<?> strategyClass = Class.forName(strategy);
                    FlippingStrategy fstrategy = (FlippingStrategy) strategyClass.newInstance();
findings.reasoning: The `strategy` variable comes directly from `req.getParameter(STRATEGY)` (an HTTP request parameter). This string is passed to `Class.forName()` without any allowlist validation, and then `newInstance()` is called on the result. An attacker can supply any class name available on the classpath. While the result is cast to `FlippingStrategy`, the `newInstance()` call itself will instantiate the class, potentially triggering static initializers, constructors with side effects, or enabling gadget-chain exploitation depending on what classes are on the classpath. This is CWE-470 (Externally-Controlled Class Selection).

evidence.notes: User HTTP parameter STRATEGY directly flows to Class.forName() and newInstance() with no allowlist validation. The cast to FlippingStrategy happens AFTER instantiation, so arbitrary classes can be instantiated.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker submits a POST request with strategy=<classpath-class> to instantiate arbitrary classes on the server
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the FF4J web console endpoint (may require admin auth depending on deployment)

findings.description: Externally-controlled class name from HTTP parameter passed to Class.forName() and newInstance() in updateFlippingStrategy
findings.code_snippet:
Class<?> strategyClass = Class.forName(strategy);
                FlippingStrategy fstrategy = (FlippingStrategy) strategyClass.newInstance();
findings.reasoning: The `strategy` parameter originates from an HTTP request (caller passes `req.getParameter(STRATEGY)`) and flows into `updateFlippingStrategy()`. Inside this method, `Class.forName(strategy)` is called with the user-controlled string and then `newInstance()` instantiates the class. No allowlist validation is performed. An attacker can supply any class name on the classpath to trigger arbitrary class instantiation.

evidence.notes: Second occurrence of the same pattern in the updateFlippingStrategy method, called from HTTP POST handling. The strategy parameter comes from an HTTP request parameter.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker edits an existing feature and provides a malicious class name as the strategy, causing arbitrary class instantiation
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the FF4J web console endpoint; target class must be on the server classpath

findings.description: Class.forName() in ConsoleServlet.init() from servlet config init parameter - not user-controlled at runtime
findings.reasoning: The class name comes from `servletConfig.getInitParameter(PROVIDER_PARAM_NAME)` which is a servlet initialization parameter from web.xml/deployment descriptor - this is server-side configuration, not user-controlled HTTP input at runtime. An attacker cannot modify servlet init parameters without already having write access to the deployment configuration, making this a false positive for remote exploitation.

evidence.notes: className comes from servlet init params (web.xml), not from HTTP request. This is configuration-controlled, not user-controlled at runtime.

findings.description: User-controlled eventUUID from URL path written to HTTP response without encoding in OperationsController
findings.code_snippet: res.getWriter().println("Event " + eventUUId + " does not exist in event repository." );
findings.reasoning: The `eventUUId` is extracted directly from the HTTP request path (`pathParts[3]`) without any encoding or sanitization. When an event is not found, this unsanitized value is written directly to the HTTP response. This is an XSS (reflected) vulnerability where an attacker can craft a URL with malicious JavaScript in the path segment, which will be reflected in the response and executed in the victim's browser. The content type is set to JSON (`CONTENT_TYPE_JSON`), which may reduce but not eliminate XSS risk depending on browser behavior.

evidence.notes: eventUUId comes from URL path parts, no encoding applied before writing to response. Content type is JSON but response body contains raw user input.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with XSS payload in the event UUID path segment; when event is not found, the payload is reflected in the response body
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the ff4j operations API endpoint

findings.description: User-controlled featureId from URL path written to HTTP response without encoding in OperationsController.featuresAsJson
findings.code_snippet: res.getWriter().println("Feature " + featureId + " does not exist in feature store." );
findings.reasoning: The `featureId` is extracted from the HTTP request path (pathParts[3]) without any sanitization. When the feature is not found, the raw value is written to the HTTP response. This allows an attacker to craft a URL where the path segment contains XSS payload which gets reflected in the response.

evidence.notes: featureId from URL path, not encoded before writing to response.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts URL with XSS payload in featureId path segment; when feature not found, payload is reflected in the response
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to ff4j operations API endpoint

findings.description: User-controlled propertyName from URL path written to HTTP response without encoding in OperationsController.propertiesAsJson
findings.code_snippet: res.getWriter().println("Property " + propertyName + " does not exist in property store." );
findings.reasoning: The `propertyName` is extracted from the HTTP request path (pathParts[3]) without sanitization. When the property is not found, this unsanitized value is reflected into the HTTP response. An attacker can craft a URL with a malicious path segment containing XSS payload.

evidence.notes: propertyName from URL path, not encoded before writing to response.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts URL with XSS payload in propertyName path segment; when property not found, payload is reflected in the response
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to ff4j operations API endpoint

findings.description: User-controlled feature/property names from HTTP requests written to HTML response without encoding in ConsoleRenderer.renderPage
findings.code_snippet: out.println(htmlContent);
findings.reasoning: The `renderPage` method builds an HTML page that incorporates feature names, property names, and other user-controlled data from the feature store (which was originally populated from HTTP request parameters) without HTML encoding. Feature IDs and property names entered by users via the console are directly interpolated into HTML. The `renderFeatureRows` and `renderPropertiesRows` methods embed feature/property identifiers that were originally user-supplied (via FEATID parameter) directly into HTML table cells and JavaScript attributes without encoding. The code flow traced by CodeQL shows user input from HTTP request flows through ConsoleServlet to renderPage and then to `out.println(htmlContent)` - a stored XSS vector.

evidence.notes: Feature names and property names from user input are embedded in HTML output. The codeflow traces show user input from HTTP parameters flowing through ConsoleServlet.doPost -> createFeature/updateFeature -> stored in ff4j store -> later retrieved and rendered into HTML. Stored XSS pattern.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a feature with XSS payload as the feature ID; when the console page is rendered, the payload is included in the HTML table row without encoding and executed in admin's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to ff4j console to create features

findings.description: User-controlled data written to HTML response without encoding in ConsoleRenderer.renderPageMonitoring
findings.code_snippet: out.println(htmlContent);
findings.reasoning: The `renderPageMonitoring` method writes an HTML page with audit rows containing event data. Event data (feature names, user names, timestamps) that was originally from HTTP requests is included in the HTML output via `renderAuditRows(ff4j, req)` without HTML encoding. The code flow shows user input from HTTP parameters (feature IDs used in audit events) flowing through ConsoleServlet to renderPageMonitoring. This is a stored XSS vector where audited feature names/user identifiers get reflected in the monitoring page.

evidence.notes: Audit rows contain event data including feature names/IDs that were originally user input. Written to HTML response without encoding.

exploit_payload.code_snippet: POST /ff4j-console HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded op=CREATE_FEATURE&featid=
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a feature with XSS payload as ID; when an audit event is generated for that feature and admin views monitoring page, the payload executes
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to create features and trigger audit events; feature ID with XSS payload

findings.description: Path traversal claim in StaticResourceController - uses classpath resource loading, not filesystem
findings.code_snippet: InputStream is = FileUtils.class.getResourceAsStream(fileName);
findings.reasoning: While the path from `StaticResourceController` does flow from user-controlled HTTP path info to `getResourceAsStream()`, the implementation uses Java classpath resource loading (Class.getResourceAsStream / ClassLoader.getResourceAsStream), not direct filesystem access. This means path traversal is limited to resources within the classpath/JAR, and cannot be used to read arbitrary files from the filesystem. The path is also validated by `resourceType` which must be one of 'css', 'js', 'font', or 'img'. This is a false positive as the actual risk is bounded to classpath resources.

evidence.notes: Uses getResourceAsStream which loads from classpath/JARs, not filesystem. Also, StaticResourceController validates resourceType (css/js/font/img) before calling these methods.

findings.description: User-controlled feature/operation error messages written to HTTP response without encoding in ConsoleServlet
findings.reasoning: The `uid` parameter comes from `req.getParameter(FEATID)` - an HTTP request parameter. It's passed to `renderMessageBox()` which constructs HTML with this value directly embedded without HTML encoding. The `renderMessageBox` method concatenates the message string directly into HTML div/span tags. An attacker can inject HTML/JavaScript by providing a malicious feature ID (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`) in the FEATID parameter. The code flow shows user input from HTTP GET parameter FEATID flowing to renderMessageBox and then written directly to the response writer.

evidence.notes: uid from HTTP req.getParameter(FEATID) flows through msg() to renderMessageBox() which embeds it in HTML without encoding. renderMessageBox does a direct sb.append(message) which is the user-controlled value.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends GET request with XSS payload in FEATID parameter; the error/success message box renders the unsanitized value in HTML

findings.description: StaticResourceController path traversal - additional file operation sinks in FileUtils also use classpath loading
findings.reasoning: These additional file operation sinks at lines 93, 96, 100, and 199 are all within the same `getResourceAsStream()` method and follow-on methods that exclusively use classpath-based resource loading, not filesystem IO. As established in the analysis of line 63, these are false positives since path traversal is limited to classpath resources, not the filesystem.

evidence.code_context: All path traversal sinks in FileUtils.java use getResourceAsStream() variants which load from the JVM classpath/JARs, not from the filesystem directly. The StaticResourceController also validates resourceType to be one of css/js/font/img before invoking these methods.
evidence.notes: False positive - classpath-only loading prevents true filesystem path traversal.

findings.description: Open redirect via unvalidated Host header in sendRedirect() call
findings.code_snippet: res.sendRedirect(req.getRequestURL() + "/");
findings.reasoning: The method `redirectUrlWithoutSlash` constructs a redirect URL using `req.getRequestURL()`, which includes the Host header from the HTTP request. Since the Host header is entirely attacker-controlled, an adversary can set `Host: evil.com` and cause the servlet to redirect the user's browser to `http://evil.com/`. The condition `req.getServletPath().equals(req.getRequestURI())` is easily satisfied by requesting the servlet path without a trailing slash. There is no validation or sanitization of the URL before passing it to `sendRedirect()`. This is a classic open redirect via Host header injection, which can be used for phishing attacks.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: req.getRequestURL() returns a StringBuffer with the full URL including the Host header. The Host header is attacker-controlled. No filtering or validation is applied before passing to sendRedirect().
evidence.notes: The trigger condition is: getServletPath() must equal getRequestURI(), which happens when requesting the servlet path without a trailing slash and no extra path info. This is easily crafted by the attacker.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
GET /ff4j-console HTTP/1.1
Host: evil.com

# Server responds with:
# HTTP/1.1 302 Found
# Location: http://evil.com/ff4j-console/
# This redirects the user to attacker-controlled domain
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a request with a crafted Host header to trigger the trailing-slash redirect, causing the victim's browser to be redirected to the attacker's domain
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The FF4j web console must be accessible; attacker needs to craft the URL with a malicious Host header and socially engineer a victim into clicking the link
